author: @TonyWeirPD
text: These aren't exactly "engines", though, are they? Basically hydraulic motors. There was an actual water engine design which used liquid water (as opposed to gas) as the working fluid. John Malone. It never really took off because, I suppose, it didn't offer any real advantages over more traditional heat engines. The nature of the thing is that the displacement is rather small and the working pressure is very high, which made it a bit useless and hard to engineer back in the day. It might be something you want to play with, though.

author: @GREENSWARMCHEMISTRYBILLIONAIRE
text: Could you make a pelton wheel,  where half of the device is sealed and full of water.  Now make air pockets under the buckets that  use the bouyancy of air to lift the buckets of the pelton wheel on the 2nd half of the pelton wheel.   One side of the pelton wheel is normal, and has gravity pulling the weight of water down, but on the other side of the device, the bouyancy of the air pockets under the buckets is lifting the buckets.   Add magnets and electric field and you have a closed loop motor that runs on the gravity of recycled water and the bouyancy of air. Would this work?

replies.author: @GREENSWARMCHEMISTRYBILLIONAIRE
replies.text: make the buckets out of a material that floats and you have even more bouyancy on the side of the hybrid pelton wheel that is full of water.      the weight of the water buckets on the normal side of the pelton wheel and all that bouyancy on the other side of the pelton wheel that is sealed and full of water.   this should be enough force to make this hybrid pelton wheel run forever in a closed loop system that recycles water.  just like a normal pelton wheel would run indefinitely with a constant flow of water   (like hydro electric dams.  this is a closed loop hydroelectric dam that recycles the water used)

author: @howardsimpson489
text:
More than a hundred and fifty years ago in Wellington New Zealand there was a city wide hydraulic system.

The good quality water came from a spring and dam high in the hills.

The wharf cranes used the water at more than 200 psi to operate rams that moved the cranes on their rails and to provide lift with sheaves and steel cables.

The coupling pipes were joined with knuckles that leaked a fine spray as they moved.

These were still in use in the 1950's where I remember them as a child.

I think one of the marine slips used the same system although the other had a still operating coal fired boiler and double expansion steam engine.

author: @davidcullen4996
text: Seems info on these is a little bit hard to come by but I interestingly came across some designs that I think replicated head/height using pressure from weight. Unfortunately Im lacking the necessary shed to do any tinkering but makes me wonder could you have a configuration based on a closed loop cycle with check valves where pressure is generated by weight in tank A which runs through the motor to a tank B at a lower pressure (due to lower weight) if the weight is on a pulley system all you have to do is shift a little bit of weight (e.g: pumping a bit of water) to cause an imbalance and generate pressure from tank B. I doubt Im explaining it very well and no doubt there is a fatal flaw but these videos are always great at getting the hamster going. Keep up the amazing work.

author: @Route72Trucks
text:
I hope you guys can weigh in on a thought experiment I’ve had.   

Invasion two large cylinders.  A chain hoist on a rail capable of being positioned over each tank.   Hoist is used to lift and reposition a large weight above each tank.   

One tank, full of water has the weight released upon it.  Water is forced through a very small aperture up a tube to a water motor located between the tanks at the top.  

Pressure created by the heavy weight drives water up the tube, through the nozzle, spins the wheel then discharges into the apposing tank, filling it with the “waste water”.  

Once the weighted tank is empty the “waste tank” is now full.  

Now The waste tank is weighted, reversing the process.  

Anyone willing to run the math on what volumes and pressures could be required to create a viable amount of electrical energy?

replies.author: @davidcullen4996
replies.text: Had similar thoughts and it seems so simple I am left thinking there has to be a fatal flaw. If you have the weight evenly balanced either on a fulcrum or over a pulley system for two large cylinders for tanks you'd only need to move a fraction of that weight to create an imbalance and thus pressure. It likely couldn't be very high pressure as it would be a free moving in the cylinder and not a closed tank but some old photos I've found seem to have ones based on huge weight/ballasts that are 10s of tonnes just by the looks of them. Unfortunately no space to tinker and Ive no clue what math would need to be run

replies.author: @Route72Trucks
replies.text:
@davidcullen4996   Yes sir.  Sense we are not trying to simply transfer the water we need more that just an imbalance.   We need a high level of pressure to create a strong driving force to act on the turbine (pelton wheel).   

The cylinders will need to be tall and smaller diameter as the weight is evenly distributed among the square led area of the waters surface.   

I’m curious how to do the math on expected run time based on available water in the cylinder vs pressure  at the tip of the nozzle and force that could be expected at the wheel.  These figures would help me understand if it’s worth while to build a scale model.   

I am 100% capable of the fabrication but don’t feel comfortable with my math.  If the available energy isn’t worth the overall project it’s kind like pissing in the wind.  

My rational mind says it’s feasible.   And forcing 400 gallons of water past a turbine at 500psi through a needle jet.

author: @ArtDecooe
text:
The exclusivity of the concept details and hardware were in the hands of engineers in previou s times because of the steel and iron needed to make them. That and the accurate machined tolerances ,which for metals required other machines machinists.
But now Robert you are onto something in that the parts needed have the tolerances,the machining is  already contained in the inexpensive strong molded parts,andthe machine weight is order of magnitude less.
This makes the engine /compressor  available to any number of people. 
So obvious it took Robert to see it.

author: @hughoneill9929
text: Very interesting to revisit some of these techniques.  In the 1940s, I was brought up in a Derbyshire village which had its water supplied by a ram that we could always hear thumping away day and night.  This continued until after the Ladybower Reservoir was in service.  However that burst a few pipes and the water was unclean for a long while.  So we carried on with our own ram powered supply, while the reservoir water was used for the milk coolers at the various farms.  I think it took about a year before the main supply was fit to use.  The ram may still be in place; I'll try to check on one of my infrequent visits.

replies.author: @robinhodson9890
replies.text: A series of hydraulic rams are often used on farms with a plentiful water supply, to raise water uphill for cattle etc. Their disadvantage is that they waste a lot of water, although that usually doesn't matter for untreated water in Northern England, where they're popular.

author: @CCoburn3
text: I like to look at old patents for technology. There are a large number of patents for water engines. It's not a dead field. At least one patent application is from 2021. But the older patents are easier to build since they did not have all the manufacturing techniques we have today.  Patents are a good place to do research for people who are interested in building on older technology.

author: @Daniel-OConnell
text: Interesting fact: Manchester's Hydraulic Power system was a public   network supplying water power through a piped network across the City . It used a system of high-pressure water pipes from three pumping stations from 1894 until 1972. Many of the artifacts from the system can still be viewed in the  Museum  of Science & Industry. It supplied motive power to many businesses,  where stand alone steam plant was impractical.

author: @tcmtech7515
text:
In theory, this is fine but in reality, the math does not work in anyone's favor.  The realistic cost to build one VS the few watthours of energy such a motor would realistically produce in any given time frame from a home water supply is dismal.    

If I did my math half right it might be able to charge a cell phone or two in a day based on typical household water usage.  (GPM x time x pressure drop x efficiency) / 2 = Watt Hours (give or take a lot) 😋

author: @silverpc4611
text: little off subject here. In the serpentine coil that you made before in video 1929. What is the minimum rpm's to that coil to produce a descent voltage and/or current?

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text:
E = Blv sin θ
E= volts
B = magnetic field strength (Tesla)
l = length of wire (m)
v = velocity (m/s)
θ = angle that the magnetic field sweeps the wire.
For the length just count the number of straight sections and multiply by the number of turns in your coil. Just ignore the round parts and the sin θ, you'll get an approximate result. Everything is constant besides velocity, so you can reduce that formula to a single factor * velocity to get voltage

author: @bunnysparklzbunnytime5117
text: Steam piston engines are deceptively simple. You can use the top and bottom of the piston as two cylinders. Steam goes in the top and pushes it down and then steam goes into the bottom pushing it back up. Fantastic little buggers. I can go on all day about steam engines and steam turbines along with all of the various equootrimonts. 3d print a stream engine for yourself and run it on compressed air. Compressed air won't melt plastic like steam or superheated steam lol
